meta.title: DATs: Why DATs Will Succeed — and Why They Won't

transcript:
Shanti Leon: Good afternoon, everyone. My name is Shanti Leon. I am VP of Institutional Partnerships at Bitwise Asset Management. This session will focus on the proliferation of Digital Asset Treasuries across capital markets this year. And joining us is an expert on the space, or maybe the expert, Cosmo Jiang, General Partner and Portfolio Manager at Pantera Capital. Welcome, Cosmo. Thrilled to have you here.

Cosmo Jiang: Hi, thank you. Nice to see everyone. Nice to talk to you, Shanti.

Shanti Leon: Great. So, Cosmo, a lot happened this year with Digital Asset Treasuries. If you could give us a quick overview of the DAT landscape today and then the role that Pantera is playing.

Cosmo Jiang: Yeah, maybe it's helpful if I rewind a little bit. This space has grown very quickly and, you know, some might say has deflated very quickly too. If I rewind back to, well even a little bit earlier this year, you know, we've done a lot of work on MicroStrategy to understand why it had traded at a premium for so long. It just seemed anathema to everything I understood as a fundamental value investor. But as we really dug in and re-underwrote it, we realized that, wow, these can be very sustainable business models if executed correctly. And by the way, maybe it actually makes sense to do it with an asset outside of Bitcoin. And so when we were approached by a really exciting management team in March that they wanted to start the first US—we were calling them MicroStrategy copycats at the time—MicroStrategy copycat here in the US focused on Solana, we got very excited and, you know, ended up investing, anchoring, bringing to life what was the first MicroStrategy copycat here in the US. Next week we were approached by Tether and SoftBank about their Bitcoin DAT, which ended up becoming 21 Partners, which we also ended up anchoring and being the largest investor in. At the time, I thought those would be the only two MicroStrategy copycats that we ever—that ever existed. And lo and behold, everyone saw the strong performance of those two stocks and, you know, became a very, very crowded space very quickly. So now there are over 80 DATs that are live. You know, there's been over $20 billion of capital raised in aggregate, excluding MicroStrategy. There are over $20 billion—over $30 billion of market cap. And so it's become a really big space. You know, at Pantera, we were fortunate, humbled to be the team that really helped jumpstart this whole trend. Sometimes I feel, you know, that with some bated breath because obviously it has caused some losses for some people as well. But we feel pretty excited about some of these becoming really big outcomes for some people.

Shanti Leon: Great. Yeah, I saw an amazing tweet by your CEO and founder, Dan Morehead, supporting the Solana ecosystem and saying, "We're skating where the puck's going." So that was great to see the buy-in from your whole firm. In terms of the best capital market mechanisms or investment strategies that a DAT can use to generate accretive yield, how do you guys think about that? How do you advise the DATs that you work with?

Cosmo Jiang: Yeah, if we go back to like what a DAT actually is, the whole goal of a public company is to maximize shareholder value. A DAT chooses to maximize shareholder value by maximizing accumulation of tokens per share. So in the case of a Solana DAT, such as our Solana DAT, Solana Company, HSDT, we're very focused on how do we maximize the number of tokens per share and in that case, Solana per share. There are a few ways to do that. In a lot of ways, these businesses are actually analogous—these business models have always existed. They just used to be called financial services companies. You know, banks, insurance companies, they are also balance sheet-based businesses whose goal is to maximize book value per share. They happen to denominate in dollars and we happen to denominate in SOL. So we're like a Solana-based financial services company that's trying to maximize book value per share. How you do that? It's sort of the tried and true methods that have been true since the dawn of capitalism, which is there's a combination of financial engineering that you can do on the liability side of the balance sheet, and then there's a combination of yield generation that you can do on the asset side of the balance sheet. And so, you know, on the liability side or on the capital—on the financial engineering side, you can go raise equity in an accretive way if you trade above book value. You can go raise debt. You can go sell warrants in order to monetize your volatility. Or sell preferreds to monetize your future value creation. And so there are all these ways that you can generate yield as defined as increasing accretive to book value per share or Solana per share. On the asset side of the balance sheet, you know, on the organic side, you can look at—especially with something like Solana—the very simplest thing is to just go stake your Solana. So we are one of the largest stakers of Solana—Solana Company, one of the largest stakers of Solana. And, you know, we try to support the validator ecosystem by diversifying across a set of validators and really in search of a balance of maximizing yield while also keeping in mind what the best outcomes are for Solana as an ecosystem. Other ways are deploying into DeFi. There's going to be some exciting announcements about that over the course of this conference that we have. As well as potentially, you know, interacting with CeFi and doing lending of Solana. So all those are really interesting things. I'd say the most important thing beyond the balance sheet is probably the number one job of a DAT, if our goal is maximizing shareholder value by maximizing tokens per share, is also maximizing the value of the token. Right? If Solana doesn't have value or if we don't believe Solana has value, then this whole thing doesn't make sense. And so we strongly believe that Solana is the most exciting L1 with the highest upside—highest, you know, risk-adjusted upside. And so we feel like Solana Company's job is to be the IR function effectively for Solana. To tell mainstream investors, to deliver that message to a whole new broad set of people, which is what we're out here doing.

Shanti Leon: You guys have been an amazing partner to the Solana ecosystem. You touched on the DATs using staking as a mechanism to generate yield. How do you see the competitive landscape between ETPs now available in the US versus a DAT that is incorporating staking?

Cosmo Jiang: Definitely. I'm often asked by allocators in my discussions with allocators like, "Why do I buy a DAT when I can go buy, you know, passive ETFs such as the Bitwise SOL ETF?" which is—plug—one of the highest performing Solana ETFs by volume. It's pretty clear to me that ETFs are passive and DATs are active. Many people can choose active management to get a different type of exposure to the underlying. It's not going to be one-to-one correlated to the underlying like a passive ETF is. So if you buy a passive ETF, you know what you're going to get. You get one Solana per share or, you know, the equivalent of one Solana per dollar you put in. And, you know, that doesn't change over time excluding some minor fees that you pay every year. With an active manager, such as a DAT, what you get is a management team that's really focused on all the different ways, all the levers you can pull both on the capital markets side and on the asset yield generation side in order to maximize Solana per share. And so you can do more than just staking. And, you know, that's not to say though that just like not every active manager beats the passive ETF, not every DAT is going to beat the ETF. And, you know, that would be par for the course for capitalism. And what I would fully expect is most DATs do not outperform ETFs, just like many active managers do not outperform the index. But high-performing DATs, high-performing active managers absolutely have a right to win and can win.

Shanti Leon: Great. You touched on maybe not every DAT making it to the finish line outside maybe the core Solana DATs. So when you think about the top operational or financial red flags, what should an institution or an investor look out for?

Cosmo Jiang: There are a few things that I'd lay out. You know, first is certainly: are management incentives in the right place? And so understanding like what the management team is really focused on, are they—what are their incentives, what are their KPIs? You know, as Pantera, as the asset manager for Solana Company, we're very focused on maximizing the amount of Solana that the company owns, but we're also the largest shareholder in Solana Company. So we're also very focused on maximizing value per share as a shareholder. And so that incentive alignment we think is really important. The second thing that you really should look for is the management team and their service providers' experience. A DAT is, in many ways—I come from the traditional finance world, I operated in private equity and long/short equity for most of my career, and so many of these capital markets, financial engineering tools are very simple or nature to me. And I'd say that's, you know, candidly not true for many crypto natives. These tools have been tried and proven in the traditional finance world, and so knowing how to approach bankers, how to approach the sell side, how to approach potential investors, delivering them the message and the product they need is very important. And not everyone really knows that message. So looking at their experience. On the asset management side, it's really important to find a management team that's really focused on risk-adjusted return. There are many ways to maximize your return. These DATs, many of them will stretch along the risk curve and become effectively actively managed hedge funds. You know, that's not to say that's necessarily a right or wrong thing, but you as an investor have to choose whether or not that's actually what you want. Whether you want to underwrite that this management team, by doing very, very black box or sophisticated strategies, whether that's something that you approve of and you're willing to trust them with. And so there's varying things. I'd say the last thing is just, you know, their—and maybe the most important thing—is their ability to get in front of mainstream audiences. The whole point of a DAT is—and as much as I love everyone that I work with and I love everyone in this crowd—the whole point of a DAT is not to market to crypto natives. Right? The whole point of a DAT is to deliver the story to a new set of investors, in particular the large pool of capital that exists in the equity markets from traditional retail investors. And so—and traditional retail investors and traditional long-only institutions. And so seeing that there's a strong marketing game trying to tackle—marketing and distribution game trying to tackle those new investors and not necessarily focused on crypto natives is pretty important.

Shanti Leon: So what are you seeing from institutions when you have conversations? Who's coming into this space? Who's investing?

Cosmo Jiang: So if we—I think it starts with, you know, if you look at MicroStrategy's top 10 holders, it includes some of the largest funds in the world. Right? Capital Group is the largest mutual fund in the US. They're—if anyone has a 401k, you probably don't know, but you're actually invested in a Capital fund most likely. And they're a top 10 holder of MicroStrategy. If you look at Norges, the Norwegian Sovereign Wealth Fund, is a top 10 holder of MicroStrategy. And so those are the types of investors that are circling these opportunities. These DATs do need to get to a scale where it becomes relevant for large long-onlys and for large institutions. And so, you know, that's a few billion market cap range. But if you can generate the momentum to get there and start building—you know, seeding the ground and having the conversations with them, which we're having with many long-onlys and other institutions to start educating them, eventually when you get to that scale, they'll be there and then when they allocate, they actually do allocate in size.

Shanti Leon: Makes sense. Okay, so on a look-forward basis, how are you thinking about DATs in the broader capital markets in five years, ten years?

Cosmo Jiang: Yeah. I love that question because almost always my conversations with people are like, "What's happening to DATs this week?" But I'd say, look, we just went through a phase of what I would call like the genesis phase of DATs. We just saw the creation of a whole new industry. You have to realize the creation of something only happens once. And so, you know, back in April there was all this white space. I had a whiteboard that wrote down all the DATs that I thought made sense, like one to two scaled ones for each of the large cap assets, you know, a few other assets that I thought were interesting. Clearly a lot of entrepreneurs saw that same opportunity and more than one or two tackled all those opportunities, as you would expect with any set of startups. Now we're at a phase where, well, all that white space, if I go back to that whiteboard, I've checked off everything. They all exist. Not to say that there won't be more, but the obvious white space is gone. And now the moats—the barriers to entry are much higher. It's going to be much harder to have a real reason to launch a de novo DAT. And so now that we're past this genesis phase, we're in the, what I would call, the consolidation and execution phase, which is, you know, pretty much most of history. And, you know, the public markets are really amazing. That's where the most value creation happens is in the public equity markets. And so we're in the part where these DATs will now need to figure out whether they are going to compete and become one of the leaders. If they are the leaders, you know, they'll be in a position to potentially consolidate the space either through acquisitions or other partnerships. So I think we're going to see a lot of consolidation. I think we're going to see the separation of winners and losers. All that is healthy because, you know, eventually the capital will consolidate into one or two leaders that can really drive the—importantly, Solana forward.

Shanti Leon: Great. Thank you, Cosmo. Absolute masterclass on the DAT space. I just want to quickly plug a blockchain letter that Cosmo had written. It's on the Pantera website. So informative. I learned a lot. I think you all would enjoy it. And to just quickly end on a personal note, what is a quote that you live by?

Cosmo Jiang: I always think back—and I always think back because I keep a daily trade journal of, you know, my thoughts, my mood, just like how I'm thinking about the day and the things we're doing. And at the top of my trade journal, I have a few things that I reread every day, like my mantras. One of the things is: always be long-term greedy. It was something that my PM at Hitchwood Capital taught me when I was a really young investor. And the idea being, you know, you shouldn't be optimizing for short-term gains, shouldn't be optimizing for short-term wins. Really always try to be long-term greedy.

Shanti Leon: Excellent. Thank you, Cosmo. Appreciate it.

# DAT’s All, Folks? What’s Next for Bitcoin Treasury Companies
![Bitcoin-2 Orange-3](https://images.ctfassets.net/h62aj7eo1csj/7bJ7UiXsu9f1h537JEh6rl/b65a9f4e630f813c254bab897837c01a/Bitcoin-2_Orange-3.png)
## Introduction
In July, Galaxy Research[argued](https://www.galaxy.com/insights/research/digital-asset-treasury-companies)that the digital asset treasury company (now typically abbreviated as DAT) model worked only as long as a company’s equity premium held. At the time, these stocks were trading at lofty multiples of their underlying bitcoin (or other cryptocurrency) net asset values (NAVs), sustained by an accretive loop between price, issuance, and accumulation.
The bitcoin treasury model is fundamentally a liquidity derivative. It works only when the equity trades at a premium to its BTC NAV. Once those premiums collapse, the entire flywheel reverses.
The core warning in Galaxy Research’s July[alert on bitcoin treasury companies](https://www.galaxy.com/insights/research/why-are-bitcoin-treasury-companies-trading-at-such-high-premiums-to-nav)and[broader report on DATs](https://www.galaxy.com/insights/research/digital-asset-treasury-companies)was that this reflexive cycle would stall once premiums tightened and equity issuance became dilutive rather than accretive. That exact scenario has arrived.
Bitcoin has fallen from \~$126k in October to as low as \~$80k (at the time of writing, it’s rebounded to around $92,000), knocking the wind out of the high-beta treasury trade. The macro backdrop has turned increas

[Small Business](https://www.forbes.com/small-business/)

# Why Digital Asset Treasury Companies Are Changing U.S. Public Markets

By [Krishan Arora](https://www.forbes.com/councils/forbesbusinesscouncil/people/krishanarora1/),

Forbes Councils Member.

for [Forbes Business Council](https://www.forbes.com/councils/forbesbusinesscouncil/)

COUNCIL POST
Expertise from Forbes Councils members, operated under license. Opinions expressed are those of the author.
\| Membership (fee-based)

Nov 13, 2025, 08:45am EST

Share

Save

_Krishan Arora, Managing Partner at_ [_Novastone Capital Advisors_](https://novastone-ca.com/) _, a PE group with over $440M in capital deployed across 23 global acquisitions._

getty

Over the [last year](https://www.galaxy.com/insights/research/digital-asset-treasury-companies), a new type of public company has begun to gain popularity across the U.S. capital markets. They are not traditional fintechs or crypto exchanges; they are something more subtle, yet potentially more transformative.

They are called digital asset treasury companies, or DATs. And they're changing how public companies think about capital allocation, balance-sheet strategy and investor engagement in the digital era.

## What Exactly Is A Digital Asset Treasury Company?

A digital asset treasury company is, at its core, a publicly listed firm that holds significant digital assets such as Bitcoin, Ethereum or other tokens on its balance sheet. Some are pure-play vehicles designed solely to buy and hold these assets, while others integrate yield strategies like staking, validator operations or collateralized lending.

What makes DATs distinct is not just what they hold, but how they operate. They utilize traditional capital market tools, such as equity, debt and structured instruments, to raise funds and deploy capital into digital assets. The result is a hybrid entity: part holding company, part market-access vehicle for institutional investors who want crypto exposure within a regulated framework.

## Why DATs Are Emerging Now

There are many converging forces that have made this the right moment for DATs to gain traction. Some of these reasons are:

### 1\. Institutional Acceptance Of Digital Assets

Bitcoin ETFs, custody regulation and broader crypto infrastructure have pushed digital assets into the mainstream financial ecosystem. Some institutional investors who once dismissed crypto as speculative are now seeking structured exposure. DATs provide exactly that—an [SEC-registered](https://www.forbes.com/sites/chrisperkins/2025/09/08/how-digital-asset-treasury-companies-stole-the-crypto-summer-spotlight/), publicly traded vehicle that bridges traditional markets and token economics.

### 2\. Inflation And The Search For Yield

In an environment of fiscal uncertainty and shrinking real returns, corporate treasuries are under pressure. Holding large amounts of [idle cash](https://www.treasurers.org/hub/treasurer-magazine/cash-conundrum) is no longer neutral—it can be a drag. Digital assets, with their asymmetric risk-return profile, have become a tempting alternative for firms seeking uncorrelated upside.

### 3\. The SPAC Revival And Capital Formation Agility

SPACs and PIPEs have [reemerged](https://www.coindesk.com/business/2025/07/08/crypto-treasury-firm-reserveone-going-public-in-1b-spac-deal) as efficient funding mechanisms for companies operating in or adjacent to crypto markets. DATs can leverage these vehicles to go public, raise capital quickly and deploy funds into digital assets—all within a matter of months rather than years.

### 4\. Narrative And Branding Leverage

In public markets, narrative drives valuation. Companies [positioning](https://www.axios.com/2025/06/03/crypto-strategy-bitcoin-digital-asset-treasuries) themselves as digital-asset-forward—such as those with balance sheets tied to Bitcoin or Ethereum—have seen premium valuations and expanded investor bases.

## The Business Model Logic

The idea behind a DAT i

tags.impact_reason: The fireside chat discusses the growing trend of Digital Asset Treasuries (DATs) and Pantera Capital's investment in a Solana-focused DAT, suggesting increasing institutional interest in Solana.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a futuristic, high-tech vault as the semantic anchor because 'Digital Asset Treasuries' directly implies a secure place for holding valuable digital assets. The story is about the 'Genesis Phase' ending and a new 'active' phase beginning, which is visually represented by the massive vault door being opened for the first time. For the homepage, I integrated the word 'GENESIS' onto a holographic display on the vault door, making it a diegetic part of the scene to directly reference the headline and the end of this initial era.
visual_idea.description: A monumental, circular vault door made of brushed titanium is the central focus, set within a cavernous, dark hall. The vault door is slightly ajar, and brilliant, volumetric shafts of light—resembling data streams—are spilling out from the crack. On the polished face of the vault, a sleek, integrated holographic screen displays the single word 'GENESIS', its light flickering as if it's about to be decommissioned. In the deep background, through the opening, we glimpse server racks with glowing circuitry and abstract representations of blockchain data, hinting at the vast digital wealth now becoming 'active'.

editorial.headline: Pantera’s Cosmo Jiang: The "Genesis Phase" of Digital Asset Treasuries Is Over
editorial.standfirst: With over 80 firms now copying the MicroStrategy playbook, the easy money has been made. The next wave of value creation will belong to "active" treasuries that function less like holding companies and more like Solana-denominated banks.
key_takeaways: The DAT sector has swelled to 80+ companies raising over $20B in capital, moving the market past the initial "copycat" phase.
Pantera has anchored two major players: Solana Company (HSDT) and the Tether/SoftBank-backed Twenty One Capital (XXI).
Future winners will differentiate via "Active Treasury" management—leveraging staking, DeFi yields, and volatility monetization rather than passive holding.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
The "MicroStrategy trade"—issuing equity to hoard Bitcoin—is no longer a contrarian bet; it is a crowded trade. Speaking at Breakpoint 2025, Pantera Capital’s Cosmo Jiang outlined a stark reality for the burgeoning Digital Asset Treasury (DAT) sector: the "Genesis Phase" is finished. With over 80 DATs now live and an aggregate $30 billion in market capitalization (excluding MicroStrategy), the window for simply announcing a treasury pivot to pop a stock price has closed. The market is shifting rapidly toward consolidation, where operational expertise will separate the institutional-grade vehicles from the "zombie" holding companies.

Jiang, whose firm anchored the launch of **Solana Company (HSDT)** and the Tether-backed **Twenty One Capital (XXI)**, argues that the next source of alpha isn't accumulation, but *accretion*. While passive ETFs offer investors a 1:1 correlation with underlying assets, a properly managed DAT operates like a financial services firm. By actively managing the balance sheet—through staking Solana, deploying capital into DeFi, and selling volatility via warrants—these entities aim to increase the book value of tokens per share. In this model, HSDT is not merely a vault for SOL; it is an active participant in the ecosystem, generating yield that passive vehicles cannot match.

This shift from passive holding to active management is critical as institutional giants like **Capital Group** and **Norges Bank** begin to circle the sector. These allocators demand more than just beta; they require sophisticated capital markets strategies that leverage traditional financial engineering to compound value. Jiang notes that for a DAT to justify a premium to its Net Asset Value (NAV), it must prove it can outpace the underlying asset itself—a feat achievable only through the "levers" of yield generation and accretive equity issuance.

However, the path forward is fraught with execution risk. Jiang warns that many new entrants lack the "TradFi" pedigree required to navigate Wall Street’s capital markets, leaving them vulnerable as the sector consolidates. The distinction between a DAT that acts as a high-beta hedge fund and one that operates as a disciplined compounder will determine survival. As the initial hype cycle fades, the market will mercilessly cull the copycats, leaving capital to pool around a few dominant leaders who can execute on the promise of an active, yield-bearing treasury.
